meta.description: MVP test slice #6. Covers Establishment Clause doctrine from Everson (1947) through Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022). Four doctrinal eras: incorporation/foundation, Lemon era (1971-2022), Kennedy overruling (2022), post-Kennedy unsettled doctrine. First slice with a fully overruled DoctrinalTest (Lemon test, valid_until 2022). Five schema issues surfaced (#29-33).

areas.label: Establishment Clause › Doctrine
areas.description: The First Amendment's prohibition on government establishment of religion. The Establishment Clause bars government from officially sponsoring, promoting, or preferring religion over non-religion, or one religion over others. For 51 years (1971-2022) this was analyzed under the three-part Lemon test. In Kennedy v. Bremerton School District (2022), the Supreme Court explicitly overruled Lemon and replaced it with a standard grounded in historical practices and understandings at the time of the founding and Reconstruction. The doctrine is currently in post-overruling transition: the new standard is stated but its operational content is being worked out in lower courts.
areas.notes: status: disputed reflects the post-Kennedy transition — the governing standard is stated but its operational content is actively unsettled. valid_from reflects Everson v. Board of Education (1947), which incorporated the Establishment Clause against the states. The Establishment and Free Exercise Clauses are textually adjacent ('Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof') and in structural tension: laws that accommodate religion risk Establishment Clause violation; laws that are neutral toward religion may burden Free Exercise. SCHEMA ISSUE D: This structural tension requires a RELATED_TO edge to free_exercise with careful justification — a different relationship type than the prior restraint/core political speech connection.

doctrines.label: Establishment Clause Doctrine
doctrines.description: The Establishment Clause prohibits government from establishing an official religion, preferring one religion over others, or preferring religion over non-religion. These core prohibitions are not contested. What is contested is how to identify violations in practice — what test or framework a court applies to determine whether a government action crosses the constitutional line. The Lemon test (1971-2022) provided a structured three-part framework. Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) replaced it with a historical practices and understandings approach. The coercion test (Lee v. Weisman, 1992) remains as an alternative analytical framework, particularly in the school prayer context.
elements: Government may not establish an official religion
Government may not prefer one religion over others
Government may not prefer religion over non-religion (or non-religion over religion)
Government may not coerce religious participation or observance
The permissible scope of government religious accommodation is determined by historical practices and understandings (post-Kennedy)
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE C: The post-Kennedy status of this doctrine is a distinct state from standard 'disputed' doctrine. The Lemon test is gone; the historical practices standard is stated but not operationalized; lower courts are producing inconsistent results. This is 'post-overruling transition' — a state where the old framework is abolished and the new framework is stated but has no settled application. Proposed fix: dispute_source: post_overruling_transition (from Issue #24) captures this, but the doctrine node itself needs a transition_status field to document that the governing framework changed in a specific year and the replacement is still being operationalized.

doctrines.label: No-Establishment Core Doctrine
doctrines.description: The bedrock prohibitions of the Establishment Clause that are not contested regardless of which analytical framework governs: government may not mandate religious observance, sponsor official prayer, require profession of religious belief, or coerce religious practice through legal sanctions. These prohibitions were settled before Lemon and survive Kennedy — they represent the uncontested core of Establishment Clause doctrine around which the contested framework question revolves.
elements: No mandatory state religion or official church
No government-mandated religious observance or prayer
No religious test for public office or civic participation
No legal sanctions for religious non-conformity
No direct government funding of religious instruction
doctrines.notes: These prohibitions represent the historical core of Establishment Clause doctrine — the practices the Clause was enacted to prevent. They are not dependent on the Lemon test or the historical practices standard and survive the Kennedy transition intact.

doctrines.label: Historical Practices and Understandings Standard
doctrines.description: The post-Kennedy interpretive standard for Establishment Clause analysis. Courts must interpret the Establishment Clause by reference to historical practices and understandings — what was understood to be an establishment of religion at the founding and, where relevant, at Reconstruction. If a challenged practice is consistent with those historical understandings, it does not violate the Establishment Clause; if it represents a departure from historical practice in the direction of greater government entanglement with religion, it may. The standard deliberately resists formalization into prongs and treats history as the primary guide rather than a structured analytical test.
elements: Examine historical practices and understandings at the founding (1791) and Reconstruction (1868)
Determine whether the challenged practice is consistent with or represents a departure from those historical understandings
Government practices with long historical pedigree are presumptively constitutional
Novel government-religion entanglements without historical precedent are suspect
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE B: The historical practices and understandings standard deliberately resists formalization as a structured test. Gorsuch's Kennedy majority explicitly declined to articulate prongs or a sequential analytical framework. The 'elements' listed above are a reconstruction of the analytical steps implied by the standard — they are not enumerated in Kennedy. This is an interpretive methodology, not a test. The schema has Doctrine and DoctrinalTest but no node type for 'interpretive methodology.' Modeled as Doctrine because it lacks clean numbered prongs, but this is an imperfect fit. The test_form field proposed in Issue #23 would add 'interpretive_methodology' as a test_form value. SCHEMA ISSUE C: The standard is stated but its operational content is actively being developed. Courts are disagreeing about which historical sources count, how much historical continuity is required, and how to handle novel contexts with no direct historical analogue.

doctrinal_tests.label: Lemon Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The three-part Establishment Clause test established in Lemon v. Kurtzman (1971) and explicitly overruled in Kennedy v. Bremerton School District (2022). For 51 years, a government action survived Establishment Clause scrutiny only if it: (1) had a secular legislative purpose, (2) had a primary effect that neither advanced nor inhibited religion, and (3) did not foster excessive entanglement with religion. The test was widely criticized by justices across the ideological spectrum for producing inconsistent results, but it survived until Kennedy because no majority could agree on a replacement.

prongs.label: Secular purpose
prongs.description: The government action must have a secular legislative purpose. A purely religious purpose is unconstitutional. Courts look to stated purposes but may look beyond them if the stated purpose is a sham.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show the government action lacks any secular purpose. Courts gave government wide latitude here — almost any plausible secular purpose satisfied this prong.
prongs.contest_note: The secular purpose prong was widely criticized as toothless — courts rarely found a government action to lack any secular purpose. Justice Scalia called the prong 'a ghoul in a horror movie that repeatedly kill[s] and refuse[s] to die' in his concurrence in Lamb's Chapel v. Center Moriches Union Free School District (1993) — he concurred in the judgment while criticizing the Lemon test. The prong's weakness contributed to the Lemon test's eventual overruling.

prongs.label: Primary effect
prongs.description: The government action's principal or primary effect must neither advance nor inhibit religion. The Endorsement Test (Justice O'Connor) reformulated this prong: the government may not send a message to nonadherents that they are outsiders or a message to adherents that they are insiders.
prongs.burden_note: This prong generated significant litigation. Courts examined whether the government action would be perceived by a reasonable observer as endorsing religion.
prongs.contest_note: The Endorsement Test reformulation (Lynch v. Donnelly, 1984, O'Connor concurrence; County of Allegheny v. ACLU, 1989) was adopted by some justices but not all. The test asked what a reasonable observer would understand — but courts disagreed on who the reasonable observer was and what they knew. The Endorsement Test and the Lemon primary effect prong coexisted uneasily for decades.

prongs.label: No excessive entanglement
prongs.description: The government action must not foster excessive entanglement between government and religion. Entanglement includes both institutional entanglement (ongoing government supervision of religious organizations) and political entanglement (government action that generates divisive political battles along religious lines).
prongs.burden_note: This prong was used primarily to strike down programs requiring ongoing government monitoring of religious institutions receiving government funds.
prongs.contest_note: The excessive entanglement prong was eventually absorbed into the primary effect inquiry in Agostini v. Felton (1997), reducing Lemon from a three-part to an effectively two-part test in practice. The fact that courts were silently modifying the test's structure without formally overruling it contributed to its eventual demise.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The Lemon test is the first fully overruled DoctrinalTest in this graph. valid_until: 2022-06-27 (date of Kennedy v. Bremerton decision). status: overruled. The test governed Establishment Clause analysis for 51 years despite persistent criticism. Justice Scalia's Lamb's Chapel v. Center Moriches (1993) concurrence mocked the test as a 'ghoul in a horror movie.' Multiple justices called for its replacement in multiple cases. Kennedy v. Bremerton finally provided a majority willing to overrule it. SCHEMA ISSUE A: This is the first instance of a fully overruled DoctrinalTest with valid_until populated. The temporal model handles this correctly, but the visualization and query layer need to ensure overruled tests are displayed differently from active ones — particularly that queries for 'what test currently governs this area' exclude overruled tests.

doctrinal_tests.label: Coercion Test
doctrinal_tests.description: An Establishment Clause analytical framework focusing on whether government action coerces religious participation. Articulated in Justice Kennedy's Lee v. Weisman majority (1992): the Establishment Clause bars government from coercing anyone to support or participate in religion or its exercise. The coercion test asks whether government action exerts direct or indirect coercive pressure toward religious participation. It was developed as an alternative to Lemon in the school prayer context and remains operative post-Kennedy as a standalone analytical framework.

prongs.label: Government direction or control of religious activity
prongs.description: The government must have directed, controlled, or sponsored the religious activity at issue. Purely private religious expression that happens to occur on government property or at government events does not satisfy this prong.
prongs.burden_note: Challenger must show government involvement in directing or sponsoring the religious activity — not merely permitting it.
prongs.contest_note: The line between government direction and government permission is contested. Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) held that a public school football coach's post-game prayers were his own private religious expression, not government-directed — even though he was a government employee acting in his official capacity on school grounds. The dissent argued this mischaracterized the coercion inherent in the coach's supervisory relationship with players.

prongs.label: Coercion of religious participation
prongs.description: The government action must coerce participation in religious activity — either directly (legal compulsion) or indirectly (social pressure, implicit pressure from authority figures, peer pressure in school contexts). Lee v. Weisman held that even subtle indirect pressure can constitute unconstitutional coercion in the school context, where students are legally required to attend and are subject to authority.
prongs.burden_note: Courts have recognized that coercion need not be direct legal compulsion — indirect pressure, particularly in school settings where students are captive and subject to authority, can satisfy this prong.
prongs.contest_note: How much indirect pressure constitutes coercion is actively contested. The school prayer cases apply a sensitive coercion standard; cases involving adult audiences apply a more demanding standard. Kennedy v. Bremerton's majority found no coercion from a coach's personal prayers — the dissent argued this ignored the realistic coercive pressure on players. The post-Kennedy scope of the coercion standard in non-school contexts is unsettled.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The coercion test was Justice Kennedy's preferred alternative to Lemon, articulated in Lee v. Weisman. It survived Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) as a distinct analytical framework — the Kennedy majority did not overrule the coercion test, only Lemon. The coercion test is particularly important in school prayer contexts where the captive audience and authority relationships make indirect pressure more salient.

cases.full_name: Everson v. Board of Education of Ewing Township
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The Establishment Clause of the First Amendment is incorporated against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment. States are prohibited from establishing a religion just as Congress is. A New Jersey statute reimbursing parents for the cost of transporting children to Catholic parochial schools on public buses does not violate the Establishment Clause because the reimbursement goes to parents, not churches, and is analogous to providing police and fire protection to all citizens.

opinions.author: Rutledge
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Rutledge's dissent argued for a stricter separation — the Establishment Clause was intended to erect a 'complete and permanent separation of the spheres of religious activity and civil authority.' Both majority and dissent agreed on the incorporation principle; they disagreed on its application to the bus reimbursement. The majority's wall of separation metaphor, sourced from Jefferson, became the most-cited passage in Establishment Clause history even though the majority upheld the program.

cases.notes: Everson is the foundation of modern Establishment Clause doctrine. Black's majority opinion contains both the wall of separation language ('The First Amendment has erected a wall between church and state. That wall must be kept high and impregnable') and the holding that the New Jersey program was constitutional. The apparent tension between the rhetoric and the result was noted immediately and has been a source of doctrinal instability ever since.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: State programs paying supplements to teachers in parochial schools violate the Establishment Clause. The Court established a three-part test for Establishment Clause analysis: (1) the statute must have a secular legislative purpose; (2) its principal or primary effect must be one that neither advances nor inhibits religion; and (3) the statute must not foster excessive government entanglement with religion.
cases.notes: Lemon is the most consequential overruled case in this graph. The three-part test it established governed Establishment Clause analysis for 51 years — longer than many sitting justices have been alive. It was explicitly overruled in Kennedy v. Bremerton School District (2022). The test survived despite decades of criticism because no majority could agree on what should replace it. Lemon remains historically important as the framework that shaped a half-century of Religion Clause jurisprudence even though it is no longer operative law.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A school district's practice of inviting clergy to offer prayers at graduation ceremonies violates the Establishment Clause. The government may not coerce anyone to participate in or support religion. Even indirect coercion — the social pressure on students to stand and maintain respectful silence during a graduation prayer — constitutes unconstitutional coercion in the school context.

opinions.author: Souter
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Souter's concurrence argued for a broader reading of the Establishment Clause grounded in non-preferentialism — the Clause prohibits government from preferring religion over non-religion, not just preferring one religion over others. This concurrence represents the non-preferentialist wing of Establishment Clause doctrine.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia's dissent argued that Kennedy's coercion test was unprecedented and that the graduation prayer was constitutionally permissible under the Lemon test (which Scalia also criticized separately). This is the first major dissent calling for a history-based approach to Establishment Clause analysis — a position that became the Kennedy v. Bremerton majority 30 years later.

cases.notes: Lee v. Weisman is the source of the coercion test, which survived the Kennedy overruling of Lemon. Justice Kennedy's own majority opinion here became the foundation of the coercion test that remains operative — and Justice Scalia's dissent arguing for a history-based approach became the majority position in Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022), authored by Justice Gorsuch. The INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES edge from Scalia's Lee dissent to Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) captures this 30-year doctrinal lineage.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A Ten Commandments monument on the Texas State Capitol grounds does not violate the Establishment Clause. The plurality applied a history-and-tradition approach rather than the Lemon test, noting that the monument had stood for 40 years without challenge and reflected the historical role of religion in American public life. Justice Breyer's concurrence in the judgment was the controlling rationale.

opinions.author: Breyer
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Breyer's lone concurrence was the controlling opinion under Marks v. United States (narrowest grounds rule). He applied a fact-specific, contextual analysis — the monument's long presence, its secular as well as religious meaning, and the lack of prior challenge counseled against finding a violation. This is the true holding of the case.

opinions.author: Stevens
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Stevens applied Lemon and the endorsement test to find a violation — the monument communicated government endorsement of the religious message of the Ten Commandments.

cases.notes: Van Orden and McCreary County v. ACLU (2005) were decided the same day on the same question — Ten Commandments displays — with opposite results. Van Orden upheld the Texas monument; McCreary struck down courthouse displays in Kentucky. The split result was widely seen as demonstrating Lemon's incoherence. SCHEMA ISSUE E: These are companion cases from the same Court reaching opposite conclusions on the same day — not a circuit split, not a DISTINGUISHES relationship, but a simultaneously-decided pair with factual distinctions controlling the outcome. The DISTINGUISHES edge from Van Orden to McCreary captures the factual distinction but not the unusual nature of same-day opposite results.

cases.full_name: McCreary County v. American Civil Liberties Union of Ky.
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Courthouse displays of the Ten Commandments in Kentucky violate the Establishment Clause. Applying the Lemon test, the displays lacked a secular purpose — the counties' repeated attempts to cure the displays after litigation began, while retaining the religious content, revealed a predominantly religious purpose. The Court applied the secular purpose prong of Lemon to find a violation.

opinions.author: Scalia
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Scalia's dissent argued the Establishment Clause was never intended to require government neutrality between religion and irreligion — the founding generation understood the Clause to bar establishment of a national church, not to prohibit government acknowledgment of a monotheistic God. This history-based reading was adopted by the Kennedy v. Bremerton majority 17 years later.

cases.notes: McCreary's specific holding — that courthouse displays revealing predominantly religious purpose violate the Establishment Clause — remains good law. But its methodology (Lemon test secular purpose prong) was overruled by Kennedy. status: limited because the Lemon methodology is gone but the result on these facts may survive. Scalia's McCreary dissent, like his Lee v. Weisman dissent, directly anticipated the Kennedy majority's historical approach.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A public school football coach has a First Amendment right to offer a brief, quiet, personal prayer at midfield after games. The school district violated his Free Exercise and Free Speech rights by prohibiting this practice. The Establishment Clause does not require the district to prohibit the coach's prayers. Courts must interpret the Establishment Clause by reference to historical practices and understandings, not the Lemon test, which is hereby abandoned.

opinions.author: Sotomayor
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Sotomayor's dissent argued the majority mischaracterized the facts — the coach's prayers were not private but conspicuous and accompanied by players and parents — and that the majority dismantled 50 years of Establishment Clause precedent without grappling seriously with the coercive realities of the coach-player relationship. The dissent also accused the majority of overstating the historical record for its new standard.

cases.notes: Kennedy v. Bremerton is the most consequential Establishment Clause decision since Lemon itself. It simultaneously: (1) overruled the Lemon test and the endorsement test; (2) established historical practices and understandings as the governing interpretive framework; (3) found a First Amendment violation in the school district's prohibition of the coach's prayers. The 6-3 conservative-liberal split reflects the ideological stakes. The new historical standard is deliberately open-ended — Gorsuch wrote that courts should look to history rather than trying to apply a rigid formula. Lower courts are actively working out what this means in practice. The case raises significant questions: whose history counts? how much historical evidence is required? how do courts handle novel contexts with no direct historical analogue?

edges.notes: The Establishment Clause — 'Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion' — is the textual source. Incorporated against states via 14th Amendment in Everson (1947).

RELATED_TO criteria satisfied: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both clauses appear in the same sentence of the First Amendment and are GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Cantwell v. Connecticut (1940) implicated both; Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022) decided both Free Exercise and Establishment Clause claims; RFRA cases raise both. (3) Complementary but structurally tensioned — the two clauses protect the same interest (religious liberty) but through opposing commands: Establishment Clause prohibits government promotion of religion; Free Exercise Clause prohibits government suppression of religion. Laws that accommodate religion risk Establishment Clause challenge; laws that are neutral toward religion risk Free Exercise challenge. This structural tension is unique to the Religion Clauses — no other RELATED_TO pair has this opposing-commands relationship. SCHEMA ISSUE D: The Religion Clauses' structural tension is different from the complementary relationship between prior restraint and core political speech. Consider adding a relationship_type attribute to RELATED_TO edges: complementary | structurally_tensioned | historically_linked.
Historical governance only. The Lemon test governed Establishment Clause analysis from 1971 until it was explicitly overruled by Kennedy v. Bremerton (2022). valid_until: 2022-06-27.

condition.context: Particularly applicable in school and captive-audience contexts
condition.note: The coercion test survived Kennedy v. Bremerton and remains operative, especially in school prayer contexts where captive audience and authority relationships make coercion analysis most relevant.

edges.notes: The post-Kennedy governing standard. Operative from June 27, 2022 forward.
Everson incorporated the Establishment Clause against the states via the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

Clarifies: Lemon established the first structured analytical framework for Establishment Clause analysis, giving the doctrine a concrete operational test. This clarified how courts apply the doctrine without narrowing or expanding its substantive scope.
Lee applied Lemon alongside the new coercion test. valid_until reflects Lemon's overruling.

Scalia's Lee dissent (1992) argued for a history-and-tradition approach to Establishment Clause analysis rather than the coercion test or Lemon. Gorsuch's Kennedy majority adopted this approach 30 years later. This is the second-longest INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES gap in the graph (30 years, compared to Holmes-Abrams to Brandenburg at 50 years).
Van Orden's plurality departed from Lemon in favor of history-and-tradition; Breyer's controlling concurrence avoided Lemon. valid_until reflects Lemon's overruling.

Complicates: Van Orden introduced significant uncertainty about the Lemon test's continued vitality. The plurality openly moved toward a history-and-tradition approach; Breyer's controlling concurrence applied ad hoc balancing. Combined with McCreary's opposite result on the same day, Van Orden significantly complicated the doctrinal picture.

Van Orden and McCreary were companion cases decided the same day with opposite results. Van Orden distinguished McCreary on the basis of: (1) passive vs. actively promoted displays; (2) long historical presence vs. recent additions made in response to litigation; (3) mixed secular/religious context (state capitol grounds, monuments from many sources) vs. purely religious courthouse context. SCHEMA ISSUE E: This DISTINGUISHES edge is technically accurate but unusual — it connects two cases decided simultaneously by the same Court, not a later case limiting an earlier one. The edge's direction (Van Orden distinguishing McCreary) is somewhat arbitrary since both were decided simultaneously.
valid_until reflects Lemon's overruling by Kennedy.

Scalia's McCreary dissent (2005) argued the Establishment Clause was not intended to require government neutrality between religion and irreligion — the founding-era understanding permitted acknowledgment of a monotheistic God. This historical argument was adopted by the Kennedy v. Bremerton majority 17 years later.

edges.citation: Kennedy v. Bremerton School District, 597 U.S. 507, 534 (2022)
edges.notes: Explicit overruling — the Kennedy majority directly stated it was abandoning the Lemon test and the endorsement test. 'This Court long ago abandoned Lemon and its endorsement-test cousin.'

Repudiates: Kennedy explicitly abandoned the Lemon test. Changed from OVERRULES (invalid Case->DoctrinalTest) to MODIFIES(repudiates). The Case->Case OVERRULES edge (kennedy_overrules_lemon) handles the case-level overruling; this edge handles the test-level repudiation. W001 cascade acknowledged: the lemon_test is ESTABLISHED by lemon_v_kurtzman which is overruled — the test is correctly marked status:overruled with valid_until:2022-06-27.

Complicates: Kennedy replaced a settled (if criticized) analytical framework with a deliberately open-ended historical inquiry. The doctrine is now more complex and less predictable than it was under Lemon — the test's content is actively being worked out in lower courts. This is the clearest instance of the 'complicates' direction in the graph: a case that replaced certainty with uncertainty.

Narrows: Kennedy found no coercion in a coach's prayers despite the dissent's argument that the coach-player supervisory relationship created inherent coercive pressure. This effectively narrowed the coercion test's scope — what counts as unconstitutional coercion in the school context is now less clear post-Kennedy.

schema_issues_found.description: First overruled DoctrinalTest with valid_until — temporal query behavior. The Lemon test is the first DoctrinalTest in the graph with status: overruled and valid_until populated. The temporal model handles this correctly at the data level, but it surfaces a query design requirement: any query for 'what test currently governs this area' must filter by valid_until: null on GOVERNED_BY edges and status: active on DoctrinalTest nodes, or it will return the Lemon test as a governing test. The validator and pipeline must enforce this. In Neo4j, the canonical QT-1 query (what test governs this area) must include WHERE t.status <> 'overruled' AND e.valid_until IS NULL. This is the first instance demonstrating that temporal filtering is required at the query layer, not just the data layer.

Interpretive methodology modeled as Doctrine — inadequate node type. The historical practices and understandings standard is not a doctrine in the conventional sense — it is an interpretive methodology that tells courts how to analyze Establishment Clause questions rather than what the answer is. It lacks the structural features of a Doctrine (substantive rule that lawyers apply) and lacks the structured prongs of a DoctrinalTest. The schema has no node type for interpretive methodology. Currently modeled as Doctrine as the best available fit, but the test_form field proposed in Issue #23 (adding totality_of_circumstances, classification_framework values) should also add interpretive_methodology as a DoctrinalTest test_form value, or the schema should consider an InterpretiveFramework node type for a future version.

schema_issues_found.location: establishment_clause (Area node) / establishment_clause_doctrine (Doctrine node) / historical_practices_doctrine (Doctrine node)
schema_issues_found.description: Post-overruling transition — status: disputed insufficient. The Establishment Clause doctrine is in a state that the current schema cannot precisely represent: the old governing test (Lemon) is explicitly overruled; the new governing standard (historical practices) is stated but its operational content is actively being developed in lower courts; multiple circuits are applying the new standard with inconsistent results. This is categorically different from status: disputed (contested doctrine with defined content) or status: active (settled doctrine). It is a third state: the governing framework changed, the replacement exists, but the replacement has no settled application yet. The dispute_source enum proposed in Issue #24 (post_overruling_transition) would capture this. The schema needs a way to represent that a doctrine is in active post-overruling development, including: (1) which cases are overruled, (2) what the replacement is, (3) when the transition began, (4) what aspects are still being settled.

RELATED_TO relationship_type attribute needed. The Religion Clauses' relationship is qualitatively different from prior restraint/core political speech. Prior restraint and core political speech are complementary — they protect the same interest through different mechanisms. The Establishment and Free Exercise Clauses are structurally tensioned — they protect the same interest through opposing commands, and maximizing one necessarily limits the other. Laws that accommodate religion risk Establishment Clause challenge; neutral laws risk Free Exercise challenge. This structural tension generates the RFRA problem and the accommodation doctrine. The RELATED_TO edge type needs an optional relationship_type attribute: complementary | structurally_tensioned | historically_linked | parallel_development. This would allow the research tool to explain not just that two areas are related but how.

Companion cases decided simultaneously — DISTINGUISHES edge semantics imperfect. Van Orden and McCreary County were decided on the same day by the same Court with opposite results. The DISTINGUISHES edge type was designed for later cases limiting earlier cases to their facts — a precedent/precedent-limiter temporal relationship. Using DISTINGUISHES for two simultaneously-decided companion cases is technically accurate (the cases do limit each other to their facts) but semantically odd: neither case distinguishes the other in the conventional sense of a later court limiting an earlier holding. Proposed fix: add a companion_case attribute to Case nodes (array of companion case IDs) for cases decided simultaneously on the same question. Alternatively, add COMPANION_TO as a new edge type for simultaneously-decided companion cases.
